card.intuition: Pairs of different elements cancel each other out — the majority element (appearing > n/2 times) always survives.
card.analogy: A vote-counting battle: every voter who disagrees with the current front-runner cancels one of their votes. If someone appears more than half the time, they can't be fully cancelled.
when_to_use: Find an element appearing more than n/2 times (majority element) in O(1) space
Online/streaming majority detection — processes each element once in a single pass
Variation: k candidates for elements appearing > n/k times (use k-1 counters)
gotchas: The algorithm only FINDS a candidate — if no majority is guaranteed, do a second pass to verify the candidate actually appears > n/2 times
Reset the count to 1 (not 0) when adopting a new candidate, because the new element IS your first vote for it
Does not work for 'most frequent' in general — only for the strict majority (> n/2) guarantee

exercise.prompt: Complete Boyer-Moore voting. The majority element is guaranteed to appear more than n/2 times.
hints: When num differs from the current candidate, they cancel out — decrement the count by 1
When count drops to 0, the next element becomes the new candidate with count=1 (handled by the first if-branch)
exercise.test_code:
assert majority_element([3,2,3])              == 3, '[3,2,3] → 3'
assert majority_element([2,2,1,1,1,2,2])      == 2, 'Majority is 2'
assert majority_element([1])                   == 1, 'Single element'
assert majority_element([6,5,5])               == 5, 'Majority 5'
assert majority_element([1,1,2,1,3,1,1])      == 1, '5 out of 7 are 1'
print('PASS')
